coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot embedded hybrid microgel from synthesis to sensing: Experimental and theoretical approach

coredata.dc:description:
Background The intellectual progress in fabricating artificial probes for selective appraisal of biologically admissible amino acids has displayed exponential growth in recent era.The neoteric era in material science has witnessed the significant application of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). However, the hybrid microgel of CQDs was less explored.
Results Herein, a highly fluorescent hybrid microgel for Tryptophan (Trp) sensing has been successfully prepared by in-situ addition of biocompatible carbon dots (CQDs) with poly-N isopropylacrylamide (PNIPAM). This bio-probe was successfully prepared by precise control over the size and shape of CQDs by effective surface passivation with PNIPAM. This inventive methodology facilitates the accurate and selective recognition of Trp. The operative features such as concentration, pH and temperature of this process were optimized to establish integrity, sensitivity, selectivity and efficiency towards Trp. The integrated hybrid microgel was effectively useful for the quantification of Trp with a wide linear range of 10 μM-500 μM, with a limit of detection as low as 0.1 μM. Significance: The current integrated system displayed desirable selectivity in the presence of comparable bio-molecules illustrating its booming performance towards Trp sensing. Further advancing the performance of developed sensor, the application of this strategy was extended for selective recognition of Trp in presence of various real water samples from different sources. The combining effect of high biocompatibility and fluorescent property of CQDs with thermo-responsive behaviour of PNIPAM provide smart hybrid gels with intriguing potential in diagnosis and hold remarkable potential in practical applications including healthcare and environmental remediation application.

(a) TGA, (b) FTIR, (c) UV–vis. absorption profile of pure CQDs and CQDs@PNIPAM microgels and (d) XPS wide-scan and (di-diii) high-resolution XPS spectra of CQDs@PNIPAM microgels.

(a) DLS studies showing variation of hydrodynamic radius with the increase in temperature for pure PNIPAM and CQDs@PNIPAM microgels, Fluorescence emission spectra of (b) CQDs, (c) CQDs@PNIPAM hybrid microgels showing the excitation dependent variations, (d) The optimized structure with frontier molecular orbital (HOMO and LUMO) of pure CQDs, PNIPAM and their complex CQDs@PNIPAM.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The intellectual progress in fabricating artificial probes for selective appraisal of biologically admissible amino acids has displayed exponential growth in recent era.The neoteric era in material science has witnessed the significant application of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). However, the hybrid microgel of CQDs was less explored.

Herein, a highly fluorescent hybrid microgel for Tryptophan (Trp) sensing has been successfully prepared by in-situ addition of biocompatible carbon dots (CQDs) with poly-N isopropylacrylamide (PNIPAM). This bio-probe was successfully prepared by precise control over the size and shape of CQDs by effective surface passivation with PNIPAM. This inventive methodology facilitates the accurate and selective recognition of Trp. The operative features such as concentration, pH and temperature of this process were optimized to establish integrity, sensitivity, selectivity and efficiency towards Trp. The integrated hybrid microgel was effectively useful for the quantification of Trp with a wide linear range of 10 μM-500 μM, with a limit of detection as low as 0.1 μM. Significance:

The current integrated system displayed desirable selectivity in the presence of comparable bio-molecules illustrating its booming performance towards Trp sensing. Further advancing the performance of developed sensor, the application of this strategy was extended for selective recognition of Trp in presence of various real water samples from different sources. The combining effect of high biocompatibility and fluorescent property of CQDs with thermo-responsive behaviour of PNIPAM provide smart hybrid gels with intriguing potential in diagnosis and hold remarkable potential in practical applications including healthcare and environmental remediation application.

The integrated hybrid microgel was effectively useful for the quantification of Trp with a wide linear range of 10 μM–500 μM, with a limit of detection as low as 0.121 μM.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The progressive era in material science has witnessed the significant application of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in biological imaging, wastewater remediation, photo-catalysis, ultra-sensing, drug delivery, lasers and phototherapeutic applications [1–9]. The low preparation cost with biocompatible nature and effective optical and fluorescence properties of CQDs have further supported the utilities in bio-sensing, catalysis and photo-emitting diodes [10–13]. CQDs are considered as one of the contemporary fluorescent probe in the area of selective and sensitive detection of harmful analytes [14–17]. Up to now, diverse range of methodologies including chemical vapor deposition, microwave assisted, hydrothermal and biological methods have been explored for the fabrication of CQDs for different applications [18,19]. For instance, Wei et al. synthesized fluorescent CQDs via the calcination method using gynostemma as precursor for bioimaging and antioxidant applications in zebrafish [20]. Shao et al. proposed the controlled synthesis of graphene quantum dot (GQD) functionalized with porous SnO2 (SnO2QNP)/ZnO nanostructure [21]. The developed composites have possessed significant role in H2S sensing. Li et al. fabricated a series of well-dispersed Cu-CuxO embedded into carbon matrix composites (Cu-CuxO@C) for phenol degradation [22]. Jusuf et al. synthesized CQDs from the ashes of eggshell membrane in sodium hydroxide solution and used it for the degradation of methylene blue (MB) [23].

In spite of latest progress in fabrication methodologies, the controlled structures of CQDs with defined quantum yield under eco-friendly conditions still persists a confrontation among researchers [24–26]. In addition, the existence of adhesive forces among small sized CQDs have caused the agglomeration in CQDs and possessed the negative impact on the active surfaces. Thus, the effective stabilizers must be employed to retain the efficiency of CQDs [27]. In this regard, microgels are considered as an effective alternative for the stability of nanoparticles. The physical barricading by the microgels has the ability to reduce the agglomeration rate in nanoparticles [28,29]. Moreover, the incorporation of nanomaterials in microgel networks has been considered as a superior alternative for modulating the physicochemical, mechanical and electrical properties of pure microgels. These hybrid microgels have been considered as more efficient adsorbents for dye removal in comparison to their bulk counterparts. The incorporation of nanoparticles in the microgel has the tendency to amend the suspension stability, diffusion rate, and thermal stability of chosen polymer cross linked network [30]. These hybrid microgels displayed the characteristic properties of both constituents and extensively employed for environmental remediation applications [31–33].

For instance, Mustafa et al. loaded silver nanoparticles in poly(N-isopropylacrylamide-co-2-hydroxyethylmethacrylate) [P(NIPAHEMA)] microgels and used for catalytic reduction of p-nitrophenol [34]. Liu et al. synthesized a folic acid conjugated poly (NIPAM-co-functional palygorskite-Au-co-acrylic acid) (FA-PNFA) hybrid microgels by emulsion polymerization for drug delivery [35]. George et al. fabricate silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) in chitosan-co-acrylic acid (CS-co-AAc) microgel to act as a sensor for hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [36]. Arif synthesized a microgel system of N-isopropylmethacrylamide (NMA) (monomer) and methacrylic acid (MAa) (comonomer) and encapsulate bimetallic (Ag/Ni) nanoparticles and used it in degradation of methyl orange (MO), congo red (CR), eriochrome black T (EBT) and methyl red (MR) dye [37]. Din et al. studied the synthesis of poly-(N-isopropylmethacrylamide-co-methacrylic acid) [p(NiPmA-mAc)] microgels for the stabilization of copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs) via free radical precipitation approach for catalytic degradation of MB dye [32]. However, the synthesis of CQDs in compact core shells/hollow shells or yolk shell of microgel has possessed less exposed surface area as compared to other nanoparticles. Partial data was available in pervious literature about the amendments in the fluorescence properties of microgels after the incorporation of CQDs and their usage in bio-sensing [38–40].

Thus, in the present research work, we explored the application of CQDs embedded poly-N-isopropylacrylamide (PNIPAM) [CQDs@PNIPAM] based hybrid microgel for the bio-sensing of Tryptophan (Trp) in aqueous medium. Trp is being an essential amino acid and highly required for the normal growth of living being [41]. Trp is required for maintaining various medical conditions like obesity, persistent headaches, depression and insomnia in humans [42]. The high dosage of Trp in the body may cause dizziness, Parkinson's disease, depressions, stroke, loss of appetite, osteoarthritis and many more [43]. Thus, precise detection of Trp is highly required for the safety of living beings [44]. The immobilized fluorescent CQDs into the polymeric microgel network offer the selective detection of Trp with high accuracy and precision. The interactional association between the CQDs and microgels was also verified by quantum mechanical simulations. The waste bio mass such as orange peels was used for the synthesis of CQDs. One pot free radical polymerization method was used for the synthesis of hybrid microgel of CQDs@PNIPAM. This methodology provides substantial fluorescent properties in the pure PNIPAM microgel that makes it a superior fluoro-probe for the detection of Trp. The combining effect of highly biocompatible and fluorescent property of CQDs with thermo-responsive behaviour of PNIPAM provide smart hybrid gels with intriguing potential in diagnosis and hold remarkable potential in practical applications including healthcare and environmental remediation application.

Orange peels were bought from sector 15 local market, Chandigarh, India. Urea (NH2CONH2) was obtained from Hi-Media. N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM, C6H11NO, 98 %) was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry, Japan. Potassium peroxodisulfate (KPS, K2S2O8, 99 %) was purchased from Merck, Germany. N, N'- Methylene-bisacrylamide (BIS, C7H10N2O2, 99 %) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and Hydrochloric acid (HCl) were procured from Fisher Scientific. HPLC grade amino acids including: aspartic acid, asparagine, glutamine, tyrosine, leucine, isoleucine, histidine, alanine, arginine, cysteine, glutamic acid, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, proline, serine, threonine, tryptophan, valine and glycine were procured from Sigma-Aldrich having 98 % purity. Double distilled water was used for the entire experimental work. Nutrient Agar and Nutrient broth were obtained from Hi-Media for the biocompatibility studies. All the chemicals were used as such without further purification. The real water samples including spring water, tap water, river water, distilled water, lake water, ground water and rain water were used for real water sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CQDs from waste orange peels
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the waste orange peels were rinsed with tap water and subsequently sun baked to remove the moisture content. The dried peels were further grinded and sieved to obtain powder. Afterwards, 10.8 g of orange peel was mixed with 5.2 g urea in 70 mL of ethanol and water (1:1) solution. The obtained solution was then processed under ultra-sonication for 4 h. Subsequently the resultant mixture was transferred to Teflon-lined autoclave reactor. The hydrothermal synthesis was executed by heating the reaction mixture at 160 °C temperature for 5 h (Fig. 1 a). After the completion of reaction, the autoclave reactor was cooled down to room temperature. The solution was further transferred to conical flask and stirred at 350 rpm for 5 h. The obtained solution was subsequently purified by repetitive centrifugation (4000 rpm for 15 min) and re-dispersion in distilled water. Eventfully, the aqueous dispersion of CQDs was collected by filtering the dispersion using 0.2 μm filter paper followed by dialysis using a cellulose membrane (MWCO 1000 Da).

The fabrication of CQDs@PNIPAM hybrid microgels were carried out as follows: Initially, 83 mL double distilled water having 1.6 g of NIPAM and 0.1 g N, N'-methylene-bisacrylamide in a 250 mL three necked round bottomed flask fitted with condenser was heated at 70 °C for 60 min under continuous N2 purging. Subsequently, 15 mL of prepared CQDs was added to the above mixture. The free radical polymerization process was initiated by adding 2 mL of potassium peroxodisulfate (46 mg) and the resultant mixture was heated for 6 h at 70 °C. Then, the obtained product was filtered, centrifuged three times at 7000 rpm for 30 min. The final product was lyophilized and stored at room temperature for further use (Fig. 1c).

The UV–vis. absorption analysis was done on JASCO V-750 spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded with Nicolet iS50- FTIR spectrometer. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images were taken on JEM-2100 Plus (JEOL) microscope. The elemental analysis and morphological studies were performed using EDX/FESEM with JEOL JSM 7500F FESEM instrument at 20 keV. Powder XRD spectrum was taken on Panalytical X'Pert Pro XRD diffractometer over 2θ range of 5–90°. The hydrodynamic radius and volume phase transition temperature (VPTT) of the hybrid microgels were calculated via dynamic light scattering (DLS) on Malvern, Zetasizer Nano-S90 laser light scattering spectrometer. SDT Q-600 instrument was used to carry out the TGA analysis. Digital Lab India pH meter was used to execute the pH experiments. Zeta-potential analysis (Anton Paar) was used to find the charge on the hybrid microgel. XPS studies were performed with a K-alpha plus XPS system by ThermoFisher scientific instruments in an ultrahigh vacuum chamber (7 × 10−9 torr) using AlKα radiation (1486.6 eV). REMI R-24 centrifuge was used for purification. The fluorescence properties of the formed CQDs and the respective CQDs@PNIPAM hybrid microgel were explored using Hitachi F-2500 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence lifetime analysis was performed on HORIBA DeltaFlex TM instrument.

Quantum yield of the prepared CQDs and CQDs@PNIPAM hybrid microgel was estimated by taking the integrated PL emission peak (excited at 330 and 290 nm for CQDs and CQDs@PNIPAM respectively) and absorbance values (at 260 and 230 nm for CQDs and CQDs@PNIPAM respectively). Rhodamine B was used as the standard reference with known quantum yield of 0.6 [45]. The calculation of quantum yield was done by using the following equation:
(1)

where ɸX and ɸR refers to the quantum yield of the sample and reference material. Ix and IR correspond to the fluorescence emission intensity of sample and reference. The absorbance of sample solution and reference is represented by AX and AR. η X and η R refers to the refractive index of sample and reference material.

Primarily, the mercuric chloride (HgCl2) solution was chosen for the sterilization of Vigna radiata seeds followed by thorough rinsing with deionized water. For the analysis, cleaned seeds were placed directly in contact with the different concentration solutions of CQDs@PNIPAM. To keep the similarities, equal amount of seeds has been placed in different petri plates having 5 mL solution of CQDs@PNIPAM. Vigna radiata seeds dipped in deionized water have been chosen as control during the studies [46]. All the experiments were executed thrice to appraise the repeatability of system. The root length was measured with the help of thread. Further details can be found in Text S-1 and Figs. S1 and S2.

The biocompatibility of the prepared samples was further evaluated using agar well diffusion method against the bacterial strains of Escherichia coli (Gram negative) and Staphylococcus aureus (Gram positive) which were revived in nutrient broth. Nutrient agar and Nutrient broth were initially autoclaved to remove any type of contamination [47]. After that, the agar was poured in sterilized petri plates and allowed to stand for gel formation. The bacterial strains were dispersed uniformly over the agar plates and the wells were formed using sterilized tips. The solutions of different concentrations of the CQDs@PNIPAM were prepared and filled the wells with the solutions leaving a blank well as a control. Further details are provided in Text S-1 and Fig. S1.

For the analysis, CQDs@PNIPAM (100 μL) was initially pipette out in volumetric flask tubes of 5 mL. 100 μL of Trp was separately mixed with the above solution. The as prepared solution was further incubated to completely react under room temperature conditions (25 °C) for 25 min. Afterwards, the fluorescence measurement of the prepared solutions was taken by exciting the sample with λ = 290 nm.

By employing the similar methodology, the selectivity studies of CQDs@PNIPAM toward Trp was executed by adding other amino acids such as: aspartic acid, asparagine, glutamine, tyrosine, leucine, isoleucine, histidine, alanine, arginine, cysteine, glutamic acid, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, proline, serine, threonine, tryptophan, valine and glycine in place of Trp. On the basis of selectivity outcomes, the interference studies were executed by adding Trp to the CQDs@PNIPAM solutions in presence of different interfering amino acids. Different real water samples such as tap water, distilled water, spring water, ground water, lake water, rain water and river water were further employed to assess the applicability of the prepared analytical method. It was also employed for real food samples such as milk, apple juice, banana juice and kiwi juice. The suspended impurities from the sample were removed by using ultracentrifugation. Afterwards, each sample was spiked with known concentrations of Trp and the recovery analysis was made for each sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational Analysis using density functional theory
ce:section.ce:para: The theoretical analysis for the prepared CQDs was assessed by employing the Gaussian 09 software. Initial focus was on the optimization of CQDs alongside their association with PNIPAM. The underlined calculations were performed by employing B3LYP functional. The interactions between the CQDs@PNIPAM and tryptophan were also validated with the computational studies. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) were attained by utilizing the Gauss-View program. Additionally, the associated parameters including energy gap (ΔEgap), dipole moment (μ), and other valuable indicators of reactive behavior such as electronegativity (χ), ionization, hardness (η), and softness (σ) were determined commissioning the DFT/B3LYP (Becke 1993; Stephens et al., 1994) method and the 6-311G∗ basis set. Further details of the DFT modelling can be found in supplementary file Text S-2.

HRTEM and FESEM images were utilized to illustrate the morphological characteristics of CQDs@PNIPAM (Fig. 1d and e). The prepared hybrid microgel of PNIPAM with CQDs has displayed the smooth surface morphologies (Fig. 1d). The existence of uniform spherical shaped particles with an average diameter of 428 ± 15 nm was clearly visualized from Fig. 1d. However, the HRTEM image of pure CQDs displayed the average diameter of 9 ± 2 nm (Fig. 1b). The size distribution profile well matched with Image J software (50 CQDs@PNIPAM measured), and was found to be 420–440 nm (Fig. S3a). EDX analysis (Figs. S3b and S3c) and elemental mapping (Fig. 1e) further confirmed the existence of C, N and O in the hybrid microgels.

The thermal decomposition for the pure CQDs and CQDs@PNIPAM has been illustrated in Fig. 2 a. The outcomes confirm that the thermal decomposition process is mainly divided into 3 parts for pure CQDs and 2 parts in case of CQDs@PNIPAM. The initial weight loss of 13 % and 20 % up to 220 °C for pure CQDs and hybrid microgel is mainly aroused due to the removal of moisture content from the surface of prepared particles. The subsequent weight loss of 47 % in temperature range of 220–340 °C has been observed for pure CQDs. However, weight loss of 70 % in the temperature range of 220–440 °C has been observed in case of CQDs@PNIPAM microgels. This weight loss has mainly been associated with the disruption of the structural networks of the samples. However, CQDs have displayed the weight loss of around 18 % between 340 °C and 700 °C. After, 440 °C, no subsequent weight loss has been observed for CQDs@PNIPAM microgels. The results support higher stability of CQDs@PNIPAM microgels in comparison to pure CQDs.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopic measurements were performed on pure CQDs, PNIPAM and hybrid counterpart for understanding the nature of existing functional groups as well as chemical interactions in the system. The FTIR spectrum of pure CQDs showed the major absorption bands at 3350, 3201, 2810, 1651, 1588, 1416, 1071, 767, and 607 cm−1 respectively (Fig. S4a) [48]. The absorption bands for CQDs@PNIPAM microgels has been observed at 3273, 2966, 1621, 1544 and 1448 cm−1 (Fig. 2b) [49]. The details of peaks along with their corresponding functional groups have been listed in Table S1. The same peaks have also been observed for pure PNIPAM microgels (Fig. S4b). The additional peaks in CQDs@PNIPAM microgels at 2856, 1544 and 986 cm−1 has further supported the effective incorporation of CQDs in pure microgel of PNIPAM. The zeta potential value found to be −8.8 mV for the CQDs@PNIPAM microgels. The diffraction patterns of pure CQDs and CQDS@PNIPAM were analyzed using XRD as shown in Fig. S5. XRD spectrum of CQDs shows a broad amorphous peak at 2θ = 27° which clearly indicates the amorphous nature of CDs. The observation of distinct broad peak at 2θ values of about 20.1° corresponds to PNIPAM [50]. The broad humped XRD peak validates the development of amorphous and short-range ordered polymer [51]. As there was a small amount of incorporated CQDs into the PNIPAM matrix, thus the XRD spectrum has not displayed any significant change in pattern in comparison to PNIPAM polymer.

The absorption spectra of pure CQDs and CQDs@PNIPAM were examined by UV–vis. spectrometer as given in Fig. 2c. The spectrum for CQDs exhibited a hump around 260 nm. The observed peak was assigned to the π-π∗ transition of CC bond in sp2 hybridization. A comparable pattern of absorption spectrum was observed in the case of PNIPAM polymer crosslinked with CQDs with a 20 nm red shift due to the effective incorporation of CQDs in PNIPAM matrix. The elemental composition of the pure CQDs and CQDs@PNIPAM was studied using XPS analysis (Fig. 2d and Fig. S6). The existence of similar peaks in CQDs@PNIPAM microgels as that of pure CQDs clearly indicates the incorporation of CQDs in the microgel network. For C1s, the peaks at the binding energy of 285.4, 287.3 and 289.2 eV correspond to the C–C, C–O/C–N and CO bonding in pure CQDs (Fig. S6a). However, these peaks were shifted to 284.6, 285.9 and 287.4 eV in hybrid microgels (Fig. 2di) [52,53]. The O1s spectra shows peak at binding energy of 532.5 and 534.1 eV corresponding to CO and C–OH binding which gets shifted to 531.2 and 532.5 eV after its incorporation in microgel (Fig. S6aii and 2diii). N1s spectra reflects peak at binding energy of 399.6 eV shifts to 399.5 eV corresponding to C–N–C bonding (Fig. 2dii and S6a) [54]. The shifting in all the peaks indicates the interaction between the pure CQDs and the PNIPAM microgel particles. The photostability of the developed system was also checked for a period of 10 days using UV–vis. spectroscopy. It was found to be highly stable with no sign of agglomeration as there was no change in the absorbance value of the hybrid microgel with the number of days (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermo-responsive and fluorescence behavior of CQDs@PNIPAM
ce:section.ce:para: The structural and size variations are one of the significant properties of thermo-responsive polymer on varying the reaction temperature [55]. The respective variations in the solvation enthalpy with reference to temperature change are mainly responsible for this kind of responsiveness in polymer matrix. The DLS studies were performed to observe this thermo-responsive behavior of prepared hybrid microgels in the temperature range of 20–50 °C. The data is helpful to estimate the VPTT and hydrodynamic radii of the CQDs@PNIPAM in reference to pure PNIPAM (Fig. 3 a). It was observed that the radii of pure PNIPAM were reduced from 495 ± 16 nm to 180 ± 11 nm with increase in temperature from 20 °C to 50 °C. The reduction in the size was mainly explained due to the collapse of polymer matrix and loss of hydration shell due to the breaking of hydrogen bonds among the polymer chains and enclosed water molecules. However, the decrement in size from 450 ± 11 to 120 ± 10 nm was observed for CQDs@PNIPAM. Additionally, the VPTT value for CQDs@PNIPAM microgels was found to be 34 °C in comparison to 32 °C for pure PNIPAM (Fig. S8). The excitation dependent fluorescence emission behavior for pure CQDs and CQDs@PNIPAM was studied by varying the wavelength from 300 to 400 for pure CQDs and 250–350 nm for hybrid microgel (Fig. 3b and c). A significant red shift was observed in the emission peak with the increasing excitation wavelength. There seems to be an increase in the intensity of the peak when wavelength increased from 250 to 290 nm. However, the intensity was found to be decreased when excitation wavelength increased from 300 to 350 nm. The maxima was found with λexc = 290 nm and λexc = 330 nm for CQDs@PNIPAM and CQDs respectively. At a particular wavelength, the fluorescence maxima of CQDs@PNIPAM gradually shift to red which may be caused by the functionalization of amine groups on the surface of CQDs. The decrease in energy gap between the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) of CQDs@PNIPAM shifts the fluorescence maxima towards higher position. The obtained quantum yield for pure CQDs and CQDs@PNIPAM microgels was 43.8 % and 39.3 % respectively.

The change in the fluorescence emission intensity of pure CQDs and CQDs@PNIPAM microgels at the excitation wavelength of 330 and 290 nm respectively was investigated by varying the pH of the both the reaction medium. The intensity of prepared samples displayed significant change with change in the pH (Fig. S9). From the data, it was observed that the maximum intensity for pure CQDs was observed at pH ∼7 in comparison to acidic and basic range. However, CQDs@PNIPAM microgel has displayed maximum intensity in the pH range of 4–10. This may happen due to the change of the electrolytic environment of the CQDs embedded in the PNIPAM network which leads to the changes in the surface structure and state of the CQDs. Hence, the fluorescence properties of CQDs@PNIPAM gets enhanced which might be due to the swelling and deswelling of the particles in different pH environments. Additionally, the highly acidic and basic medium has caused the decrement in the fluorescence intensity for both the samples. The outcomes supported the higher working efficiency of CQDs@PNIPAM in wide pH range in comparison to the pure CQDs.

The effect of temperature on the fluorescence behavior of CQDs and CQDs@PNIPAM microgels was also studied in the temperature range of 30–70 °C (λexc = 330 and 290 nm for CQDs and CQDs@PNIPAM). From the data, it was observed that for the pure CQDs, there was a linear decrease in the emission intensity which may be attributed to the aggregation of CQDs or due to the synergistic effect between the hydrogen bonds and abundant oxygen containing functional groups but there is a steep decrease in the emission intensity of the hybrid microgels with the increase in temperature of the solution (Figs. S9c and S9d). This could be explained through the swelling nature of the microgels at lower temperature due to the H bonding with water molecules that provides exposure to the embedded CQDs embedded in the microgels matrix. However, at the higher temperature range, the microgel particles shrink due to the hydrophobic interactions causing a reduction in the fluorescence intensity of the sample.

The intermolecular binding association of pure CQDs with PNIPAM was further visualized by carrying out the DFT studies. The Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) gap were calculated (Fig. 3d). The frontier molecular orbitals of pure CQDs, PNIPAM and CQDs@PNIPAM are represented in Fig. 3d and their π and π∗ orbitals are represented by HOMO and LUMO orbitals respectively. The optimized configuration showed that the band gap values of pure CQDs, pure PNIPAM and CQDS@PNIPAM were found to be 0.033 eV, 0.203 eV and 0.022 eV respectively (Fig. 3d). On interpreting the results, it was observed CQDs@PNIPAM complex exhibited a lower ΔE value in comparison to both the initial substrates. This reduction in the band gap value has also provided the additional evidence for the formation of stable complexation between CQDs and PNIPAM in hybrid microgel. Moreover, it was additionally noted that the interaction between CQDs and PNIPAM exhibited more stability as evidenced by the observed reduction in binding energy after complexation between CQDs and PNIPAM (Fig. 3d). Henceforth, it can be deduced that there exists a favorable intermolecular attraction between CQDs and PNIPAM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Propensity of CQDs@PNIPAM microgels towards tryptophan (Trp) sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence properties possessed by hybrid microgels of CQDs@PNIPAM make it suitable candidate for bio-sensing application. For the specific analysis, the incubation time was kept constant for 2 min with λexc = 290 nm and slit width 10 nm at room temperature conditions (25 °C). To attain the selectivity, the respective emission intensity of equi-volume mixture of CQDs@PNIPAM was measured in the presence of different amino acid: aspartic acid, asparagine, glutamine, tyrosine, leucine, isoleucine, histidine, alanine, arginine, cysteine, glutamic acid, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, proline, serine, threonine, tryptophan, valine and glycine (Fig. 4 a). The significant fluorometric response with enhancement of 83.9 % was observed in case of Trp while rest of the amino acid displayed nominal response with CQDs@PNIPAM (Fig. 4a). Notably, tryptophan could serve as a donor of hydrogen. Therefore, the surface-active groups provided the possibility for Trp to interact with CQDs@PNIPAM and form a CQDs@PNIPAM-Trp complex. It may be due to the stronger hydrogen bonding interactions between H present on Trp and the N present on the surface of the probe which leads to the change in the fluorescence intensity (supported by DFT results). However, no fluorescence change was observed for pure CQDs in presence of Trp (Fig. S10). The effective aptitude of the developed sensor was further investigated in presence of different concentrations of Trp within the range of 10 μM–500 μM (Fig. 4b).

There is a linear increase in the intensity of the emission peak with increase in the concentration of Trp in the reaction medium. Thus, the developed sensor acts as a turn-on fluorescence sensor for the chosen amino acid. The respective Stern-Volmer plot was used to calculate the limit of detection (σ) and quantitation limit (β) and it was found to be 0.1 μM and 0.4 μM respectively (Fig. 4c). The value of binding constant was found to be 212.1 μM using Benesi-Hilderbrand plot (Fig. 4d). The UV spectra for CQDs@PNIPAM in absence and presence of Try were also shown in Fig. S11. It shows that the absorption spectrum of CQDs@PNIPAM increases in the same pattern even after introducing the Trp solution but with a slight red shift. Also, the value of quantum yield for CQDs@PNIPAM in presence of Trp was calculated to be 63.2 % which was high in comparison to bare CQDs and CQDs@PNIPAM microgels. The fluorescence lifetime analysis was also performed to determine the average fluorescence lifetime for CQDS, CQDS@PNIPAM and CQDS@PNIPAM@Trp. The lifetime analysis revealed an average fluorescence lifetime of 2.8, 1.9 and 1.9 ns for CQDS, CQDS@PNIPAM and CQDS@PNIPAM@Trp respectively (Fig. 4f). There is no change in the fluorescence lifetime of the sensor in the presence and absence of the Trp.

The selectivity of the developed sensor was further tested in presence of different amino acids. Hence apart from Trp, other amino acids i.e., asparagine, glutamine, tyrosine, leucine, isoleucine, histidine, alanine, arginine, cysteine, glutamic acid, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, proline, were directly mixed with Trp to inspect their considerable consequence on the emission intensity of CQDs@PNIPAM (Fig. 4e). The outcomes clearly showed that the intensity enhancement occurs even in the presence of other amino acids which confirm the non-interfering abilities of other amino acids on analysis of Trp. The fluorescence emission displayed superior selectivity for Trp even in presence of higher concentration of interfering species, thus supporting the admirable selectivity of prepared CQDs@PNIPAM towards Trp.

The sensing mechanism was further assessed by carrying out the theoretical studies on CQDs@PNIPAM, Trp and CQDs@PNIPAM complex with Trp. The electronic configuration and band gap values were calculated for all the systems. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , this spatial orientation of Trp around CQDs@PNIPAM accounts for the fluorescence enhancement of CQDs@PNIPAM spectra by Trp, resulting in the decrease in band gap value to 0.021 eV (Fig. 5). Furthermore, the CQDs@PNIPAM and Trp complex have exhibited the O–H hydrogen bond interaction with bond length of 4.743 Å. The observed hydrogen bonding has further supported the complex formation with hybrid microgel. In addition, global descriptors derived from frontier molecular orbital energies, including the electrophilicity index, maximum charge transfer capability, global hardness, and electronic chemical potential, yield valuable insights into the inherent physical and physicochemical attributes for CQDs@PNIPAM and Trp complex. Consistent with Koopmans Theorem, the Ionization energy (I) and electron affinity (E) are expressed mathematically in relation to the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) energies [56–58]. Furthermore, the global descriptors including (IP), electrophilicity index (ω), and the maximum charge transfer index (ΔNmax) and T encompass the electronic chemical potential (χ), chemical softness (σ), global hardness (η), ionization potential for CQDs@PNIPAM in absence and presence of Trp has been illustrated in Table 1 [59,60]. The global reactivity descriptors derived from the equations originating from the aforementioned calculations, are systematically are also presented in Table 1. On interpreting the data, it was also observed that the CQDs@PNIPAM complex with Trp produced the reduction in E(Hartree) from −2076.483 to −2762.864. The outcomes support the stable complexation between Trp and CQDs@PNIPAM system.

The applicability of the developed sensor was further checked for various real water samples including: tap water, distilled water, spring water, ground water, lake water, rain water and river water. The fluorescence intensity has been recorded for the solutions containing hybrid microgels and Trp solution which was prepared in different sources of water via excitation wavelength of 290 nm (Fig. 6 a). There seems to be a noticeable enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of the microgels solution in all the real water samples which shows the applicability of the developed sensor for all types of water samples. The recovery percentage was varied in the range from 88 to 132 %. Sensor applicability was also checked for real food samples which include apple juice, kiwi juice, banana juice and milk. There seems to be enhancement in the intensity of the sensor as it detects the presence of tryptophan in different samples with recovery percentage of 30 %–81 % (Fig. 6b). The outcome shows the important role of the developed sensor in real sample analysis.

Various sensors have been developed till now for the sensing of Tryptophan. Table 2 shows the comparison of previously synthesized Trp sensor with the current work. Mainly electrochemical based sensors were quite common for the detection of Trp in comparison to fluorescence based sensors. However, in reference to fluorescence-based analysis, the electrochemical sensing of Trp requires pre-treatment method such as coating of the electrode surface with probe. For instance, Wang et al. synthesized a β-Cyclodextrin/Fe3O4 hybrid magnetic nano-composite modified glassy carbon electrode for Trp sensing with LOD value of 0.5 μM [61]. The modified electrode need to be air dried for few hours before performing the analysis. Pundi et al. [62] produced a chiral carbazole based sensor for the sensing of Trp with LOD value of 0.3 μM. The fluorescent probe made from europium functionalized metal-organic was employed for detecting L-Trp with LOD value of 0.3 μM [63]. Zhang et al. utilized 1,8-pyrenedione as turn-on fluorescent probe for Trp sensing (LOD = 0.1 μM) [64]. A lanthanide metal–organic framework have been developed by Su et al. for the sensing of Trp with LOD of 0.8 μM [65]. Zhang et al. [66] applied hydrothermal carbon spheres (HTCs) functionalized by the 3-aminobenzeneboronic acid (3-APBA) for Trp determination with LOD value of 5 μM. Li et al. used pyridine derived carbon dots for sensing Trp with LOD value of 5.7 nM. However, the detection range of the developed sensor was very narrow i.e. 0.02–20 μM [67]. He et al. done electrochemical detection of Trp detection using Cu2O–ERGO/GCE with LOD value of 0.01 μM [68]. Similarly, Zhang et al. produce a water stable metal–organic framework for fluorometric detection of Trp with LOD value 42.9 nM [69]. Although, there are materials which show very low detection limit, but the synthesis of sensing probe is a multiple step, laborious and took lot of time to prepare the sensory probe. Here, in the present work, we have developed CQDs embedded PNIPAM microgel system for the fluorescence sensing of Trp with a better LOD value of 0.1 μM. The developed method is quick and straightforward. The fabrication of CQDs from waste resources have further supported the biocompatibility of the developed probe and offered a better alternative to manage landfill issues. Additionally, the broader detection range, lower detection limit and its applicability to real samples with high recovery percentage has also offers notable advantages over the reported literature. The developed sensor exhibits high biocompatibility rendering it safe for practical usage. Moreover, the available fluorescent based sensor in literature is not thermo-responsive in nature. However, the developed hybrid based sensor in this work offers good thermo-response Trp detection, and supports the novelty of this work. The simplicity of the proposed work enhanced the scope of developed sensor in Trp sensing with much complicated processing.

To summarize, we report a waste orange peels derived CQDs and its hybrid microgel with PNIPAM. In comparison to bare CQDs, the corresponding hybrid microgel of CQDs@PNIPAM displayed the effective sensing aptitude towards Trp. PNIPAM increases functional groups on the surface of the CQDs responsible for more hydrogen interactions with Trp enhancing its fluorescence intensity as depicted from strong hydrogen bonding interactions approved from DFT analysis. The synthesized hybrid microgel exhibits an increased VPTT (34 °C) in comparison to pure PNIPAM microgel (32 °C). Based on the sensor with well-defined surface chemistry, superior stability, in presence of PNIPAM has further supported the effectiveness of the developed sensor. The developed CQDs@PNIPAM exhibit superior fluorescence performance and biocompatible nature towards gram positive and gram-negative bacterial strains. Using the developed hybrid microgel based sensor in fluorometric detection of Trp, achieved the lowest limit of detection i.e., 0.1 μM. The formed nanosensor exhibited outstanding selectivity towards Trp. The complexation between CQDs@PNIPAM and Trp was determined using DFT studies. This essential identification of amino acid using hybrid microgel will inaugurate innovative way in biosensing with quick response rate.
